header.title: Models
header.subtitle: Role of Daily, Micro, SL, and Total, their place in the signal, and how to read them on screen.

meaning.title: What it means
meaning.intro: A short map of the full chain.
paragraphs: The layers run in sequence: Daily -> Micro -> SL.
Daily sets the base day scenario, Micro works only inside a [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]], and SL adds execution-risk assessment.
Total is the final answer of the whole chain.

rows: Sets the base day scenario: growth, sideways, or decline.
Shows the base scenario from which signal assembly starts.
Determines whether a sideways day contains micro-growth, micro-decline, or no local tilt.
Shows whether the sideways day has a local tilt.
Checks execution risk on top of an already assembled direction.
Shows how risky the already assembled signal is to execute.
Combines the Daily answer, the Micro-model outcome, and the execution-risk assessment into one result.
Provides the final answer used by trading logic.

terms.description: The base layer that first classifies the day as growth, sideways, or decline.
A layer used only for sideways days. It shows micro-growth, micro-decline, or no local tilt.
The execution-risk layer. It compares early stop-loss risk with the chance of reaching take-profit.
The result of the Daily -> Micro -> SL chain. This answer feeds final metrics and trading logic.
The intermediate result after the Daily answer has been adjusted by the micro model for a sideways day.
A day state where Daily does not see a clear edge toward growth or decline. The Micro model can activate only in this zone.

reading.title: How to read it in the UI and metrics
reading.intro: How to read each layer on the statistics screen.

daily.title: Daily model
paragraphs: The Daily model sets the base scenario of the day. It is the layer that decides whether the system sees [[models-daily-up|growth]], [[models-daily-down|decline]], or a [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]].

If Daily leaves the day in a [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]], the chain passes it to the Micro model. If Daily already sees growth or decline, the next layer does not change that base scenario.

To judge Daily fairly, compare its result on the familiar part of history ([[models-daily-train|Train for Daily]]) with its result on new days ([[models-daily-oos|OOS for Daily]]). If it looks much better on familiar days, the layer may be too tightly fitted to the past.

rows: Daily sees a workable edge toward growth.
Daily does not produce a confident day direction. Inside such a day, the micro model may show micro-growth, micro-decline, or no local tilt.
Daily sees a workable edge toward decline.

terms.description: A scenario where Daily sees growth as the more likely meaningful day outcome.
A scenario where Daily sees decline as the more likely meaningful day outcome.
The minimum meaningful move threshold. It separates tradeable day movement from noise and affects whether a day remains sideways.
Daily-model quality on the training segment. It is useful for learning diagnostics, but it does not prove transfer to new days by itself.
Daily-model quality on unseen days after the train boundary. This is the main honest slice for transfer quality.

micro.title: Micro model
paragraphs: The Micro model exists only for the days where Daily left a [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]]. It shows [[models-micro-up|micro-growth]], [[models-micro-down|micro-decline]], or no local tilt.
If Daily already produced growth or decline, the Micro model does not intervene.

Its quality is measured only on sideways days and should be read together with [[models-micro-coverage|sideways-day coverage]]. If there are too few such days, the conclusion stays local and does not describe the full system.

terms.description: Local growth inside a day that Daily left as sideways. It is not a new day scenario.
Local decline inside a day that Daily left as sideways. It appears only as a refinement inside the sideways regime.
The number and share of days where the micro model was allowed to operate. Without enough coverage, its quality cannot be honestly generalized to the full pipeline.

sl.title: SL model
paragraphs: The SL model evaluates not direction, but execution risk on top of an already assembled signal.
A strong direction signal still does not make the trade easy in risk terms.
High [[models-sl-prob|early stop-loss probability]] or a [[models-risk-day|risk day]] flag means size and execution rules should be read more strictly.

rows: The higher it is, the more cautiously the already assembled direction should be read.
A flag that the day requires stricter risk reading even when direction is strong.

terms.description: An estimate of how likely the trade is to hit stop-loss before it reaches take-profit.
The protective exit level that closes the position when price moves against it.
The planned profit-taking level at which the trade closes in its intended direction.
A day the SL model considers dangerous by execution profile. Execution rules usually become stricter on such days.

logic.title: How it works logically
logic.intro: Where the final answer changes along the chain.
paragraphs: Daily sets the base scenario. If the day stays sideways, Micro adds a local tilt. Then the SL model evaluates execution risk and the chain produces Total.
Comparing Daily, [[models-day-micro|Daily + Micro]] and Total shows where the final answer changed: at the base scenario, at the micro tilt, or at execution risk.

The chain stays honest only if the calculation uses data that was already known at decision time. That boundary is what [[models-asof|as-of slice]] and [[models-causal-input|causal input]] are for. If later information leaks into the model, the meaning of downstream metrics breaks.

terms.description: A fixed time boundary up to which data is allowed to be known at forecast time. It separates permitted past from forbidden future.
The feature set the model was actually allowed to use at decision time. If post-fact information enters this set, the forecast stops being honest.

contract.title: Contract and limits
contract.intro: Each layer has its own responsibility boundary and its own set of prohibitions.

daily.title: Daily-model contract
paragraphs: The Daily model must rely only on [[models-causal-input|causal input]] and produce the base day scenario without post-fact hints.
If the layer looks much better on familiar history than on new days, the cause is usually in features, the history boundary between training and evaluation, or an overly tight fit to the past.

micro.title: Micro-model contract
paragraphs: The Micro model is only allowed inside the [[models-daily-flat|sideways scenario]]. It must not override a clear growth or decline scenario already chosen by the Daily model.
The layer only counts as useful if it improves the sideways subset and does so on enough [[models-micro-coverage|sideways-day coverage]].

sl.title: SL-model contract
paragraphs: The SL model runs on top of an already assembled direction and looks at the risk of [[models-sl-stop-loss|stop-loss]] before [[models-sl-take-profit|take-profit]].
Its responsibility is the execution-risk assessment of a ready signal. It must not invent a new direction from zero.

total.title: Final-signal contract
paragraphs: Total combines the Daily answer, the Micro-model outcome, and the execution-risk assessment into one result.
If the screen shows only one final block, the main reading should go through Total.

pipeline.title: Pipeline and data contract
paragraphs: The full calculation must use only the data that was already available at forecast time. Future knowledge must not reach the model before the window closes.
If later data enters the calculation, even pretty metrics stop being trustworthy.

checks.title: Which checks catch errors
checks.intro: These checks help separate real model value from pretty but false statistics.

daily.title: Daily-model checks
daily.why: If the base layer looks good only on familiar history, later assembly steps cannot fix that. They only hide a weak foundation.
daily.example: If Daily looks strong on familiar history but clearly weaker on new days, the first checks are features, the history boundary between training and evaluation, and whether the layer was too tightly fitted to the past.

micro.title: Micro-model checks
micro.why: Without [[models-micro-coverage|sideways-day coverage]], the micro layer can look stronger than it is simply because it was evaluated on a tiny subset.
micro.example: If the micro metric improved but ran on very few days, that is not enough reason to claim the whole pipeline improved.

sl.title: SL-model checks
sl.why: A risk layer is only useful while it really separates direction from execution risk instead of suppressing everything.
sl.example: If drawdowns fall after the SL-model step and useful days do not disappear en masse, the execution-risk assessment is adding value.

total.title: Final-signal checks
total.why: Total is what appears in the final numbers on the screen. If it is assembled incorrectly, the result is distorted no matter how good the separate layers look.
total.example: If Daily and the Micro model both look sensible but the final answer behaves oddly, inspect the merge into Total first.

pipeline.title: Pipeline and data checks
pipeline.why: If the calculation uses data the system could not know yet, the whole screen becomes doubtful rather than one local metric.
pipeline.example: If the model received a field that becomes known only after the day closes, a good result on new days no longer proves honest quality. The error happened before the forecast was even calculated.

splits.title: Splits
splits.description: Canonical reading of Train / OOS / Recent / Full without mixing it with the model contract.

truthfulness.title: Data truthfulness
truthfulness.description: Boundary between what was known before the decision and what became known later.

tests.title: Tests and barriers
tests.description: Checks that stop false statistics before they reach the report.

stats.title: Model statistics
stats.description: The metrics screen whose numbers are interpreted through this page.

header.title: BASE / ANTI-D branches
header.subtitle: Base execution mode and protective reversal mode for some risk days.

overview.title: Why branches exist
paragraphs: A branch is an execution mode for an already assembled model signal. [[branches-base|BASE]] keeps the original trade side, while [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] may flip it on some risk days.
[[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] turns on only where original execution looks more dangerous and the filters confirm that a reversal is appropriate.

rows: Executes the original side with no reversal.
May flip the trade side on a confirmed [[branches-risk-day|risk day]].
Useful when the reversal reduces risk better than plain execution.

terms.description: The set of trading rules: size, leverage, exit levels, skip conditions, and other execution constraints.
Execution mode for the shared model signal on top of the same trading rules.
The base mode without side reversal. It is the comparison point for protective branches.
The anti-direction branch. It may reverse the trade side if the risk day and guard filters agree that doing so is safer.

overview.why: Without two branches, one final number does not show whether the protection truly helped or only changed execution by chance.
overview.example: If [[branches-base|BASE]] earns a bit more but [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] cuts the heavy tail in drawdown, the anti-direction branch adds real risk value rather than cosmetic variation.

base.title: BASE
paragraphs: [[branches-base|BASE]] is the base execution mode with no side reversal.
BASE is the comparison point. Without it, there is no way to tell whether ANTI-D improves the risk profile or merely changes the trade path.

terms.description: The trade side produced before branch logic: long for a positive scenario and short for a negative one.

base.why: Every protective branch must be compared against BASE, otherwise it is impossible to separate true protection value from noise.
base.example: If a protective branch looks good in isolation but barely beats [[branches-base|BASE]] on drawdown and execution quality, the extra complexity is not paying for itself.

anti.title: ANTI-D
paragraphs: [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] is a protective execution branch for some [[branches-risk-day|risk days]]. It flips the side of an already assembled signal when original execution looks more dangerous.

ANTI-D does not search for a new market scenario and does not argue with the model about the day direction. It answers only the execution question: keep the original side or enter through [[branches-anti-direction|anti-direction]].

terms.description: A side reversal: long becomes short and short becomes long.
A day the system considers dangerous by execution profile. Only such days may qualify for ANTI-D.
ANTI-D is applied before trade simulation and changes the entry side itself.

anti.why: ANTI-D is only useful while it stays a targeted protection on risk days. If it starts flipping everything, the link between model signal and execution is lost.
anti.example: If the branch activates only on heavy-risk days and reduces drawdown without collapsing trade activity, that is a sign of useful anti-direction protection.

conditions.title: ANTI-D activation conditions
paragraphs: ANTI-D turns on only when all protective conditions pass together. If even one condition fails, the branch stays in [[branches-base|BASE]].
Anti-direction is a fail-closed protection. It only activates when risk is confirmed, the move range is sane, and the liquidation buffer is still wide enough.

rows: Without confirmed risk, no reversal is needed.
Filters out days that are too quiet or too extreme.
Blocks reversal when the risk buffer is too thin.

terms.description: A guard that checks whether the expected move is neither too small nor too extreme for anti-direction execution.
The remaining adverse room before liquidation after the flip. If the buffer is too small, ANTI-D is not allowed.
ANTI-D is only allowed with a valid final leverage. A protective branch must not create a broken risk profile.

conditions.why: These hard conditions fix the cause, not the symptom: they stop the branch from activating on noise and later pretending that randomness was useful protection.
conditions.example: If the day is risky but the liquidation buffer is too thin, the correct behavior is to fall back to [[branches-base|BASE]], not to attempt a hopeful reversal.

usage.title: How to read results
paragraphs: Read [[branches-base|BASE]] and [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] through the full metric set rather than one pretty number. [[branches-pnl|PnL]], [[branches-maxdd|MaxDD]], traded-day share, and skipped-day share should sit side by side.
ANTI-D is useful only when it improves the risk profile in a meaningful way. Higher profit with worse drawdown, liquidations, or execution quality does not make the branch automatically good.

terms.description: The total return of the branch on the chosen slice.
Maximum equity drawdown. It shows the worst historical drop the branch went through.
The share of traded days and skipped days. It shows whether a pretty result came from shutting the strategy down too often.
The main comparison table across policies and branches where the metric package for branch reading is assembled.

usage.why: One number hides cause too easily. A metric package shows whether the branch improved the risk profile or merely changed the return path at random.
usage.example: If [[branches-anti|ANTI-D]] gives up a little [[branches-pnl|PnL]] but clearly wins on [[branches-maxdd|MaxDD]] without killing [[branches-trade-share|trade activity]], it may be the stronger branch as a risk decision.

header.title: Train / OOS / Recent / Full
header.subtitle: History is split so the page can show where the model learned, how it behaves on new days, what shape it is in now, and how stable it looks across the long archive.

overview.title: Why splits exist
paragraphs: One model result cannot be read honestly from a single slice. The page separates familiar history, new days, the recent window, and the full archive.

On this page those four slices are called [[splits-train|Train]], [[splits-oos|OOS]], [[splits-recent|Recent]], and [[splits-full|Full]]. One good result across the whole archive still does not guarantee the model looks equally good on fresh days.

rows: What was the model trained on?
How does it behave on new data?
Last 240 trading days in the available history.
How relevant is the model right now?
The full available history: train + oos.
How stable is it over a long horizon?

terms.description: The part of history the model trained on and tuned itself to.
The out-of-sample segment after the train boundary. It is used for honest evaluation on unseen days.
The last sliding window of history used to read current model form.
The complete available history with both train and oos together.
The canonical split boundary that separates training history from unseen evaluation days.
The day-key contract used to enforce the split boundary without mixing training and evaluation days.

overview.why: Split separation stops familiar history from being mistaken for an honest check on new days.
overview.example: If [[splits-train|Train]] looks excellent but [[splits-oos|OOS]] is much weaker, the model reads better on familiar history than on new days.

train.title: Train
paragraphs: [[splits-train|Train]] is the familiar part of history where the model was tuned.
This slice shows what the model managed to learn from the past, but it does not prove the same quality on new days.

terms.description: A mode where only train days before the split boundary are used.

train.why: Train is useful for reading the learning result, but it does not show how the model behaves outside familiar history.
train.example: Strong [[splits-train|Train]] without confirmation on [[splits-oos|OOS]] means the model describes the past well, but that is not yet proof of quality on new days.

oos.title: OOS
paragraphs: [[splits-oos|OOS]] contains the new days after the [[splits-boundary|train boundary]] that the model did not see during tuning.
This is the slice that shows how the model behaves outside familiar history.

terms.description: A hard split contract with no quiet exceptions between train and oos.

oos.why: OOS removes the easiest shortcut: here the model can no longer rely on days it already saw during tuning.
oos.example: If [[splits-oos|OOS]] collapses, the first suspects are the history boundary, future data leaking into the input, or a real regime change, not a pretty [[splits-train|Train]] score.

recent.title: Recent (last 240 days)
paragraphs: [[splits-recent|Recent]] shows the model on the last 240 trading days. This slice is about current form, not about the average across the full archive.
If recent is much weaker than full-history, older history is supporting a nice average while the current market has already changed more than the full sample reveals.

terms.description: The last 240 trading days counted backward from the latest available date.

recent.why: Recent surfaces current degradation before it disappears inside a long average.
recent.example: If [[splits-full|Full]] still looks decent while [[splits-recent|Recent]] is already falling, the model is weakening in the current market specifically.

full.title: Full history
paragraphs: [[splits-full|Full]] is useful for long-horizon stability: rare regimes, long cycles, and the overall shape of the strategy across the whole archive.
But this slice must always be read together with [[splits-recent|Recent]]. A strong full-history result must not calm the reader if current form is already deteriorating on fresh days.

terms.description: The complete available history used to read stability across rare and distant market regimes.

full.why: Full history is needed so a short local success is not mistaken for proven long-term stability.
full.example: If [[splits-recent|Recent]] is temporarily weak while [[splits-full|Full]] remains strong, the right response is to study the current regime more deeply, not to discard the model automatically.

header.title: Time and trading days
header.subtitle: How the project defines the trading day, the exit moment, and the historical time boundary.

overview.title: NYSE trading day
paragraphs: The project's trading day follows the [[time-nyse-session|NYSE regular session]], not UTC midnight. That session defines entry, exit, and split boundaries for the day model.
The same UTC date can belong to different trading days in the project. For the model and reports, the NY business day matters more than the calendar date itself.

terms.description: The main New York Stock Exchange session: open at 09:30 and close at 16:00 in America/New_York.
The daylight-saving-time shift in New York that changes the UTC timestamps of entry and exit.
The UTC entry timestamp of the trading day. The rest of the window is measured from this point.
The New York business day key the project uses as the semantic trading date.

overview.why: If time is read only through UTC calendar dates, it becomes easy to break the real cause of an honest split and assign a signal to the wrong trading day.
overview.example: The same UTC date can belong to different NY business days depending on the season. Both UI and calculation must rely on the trading day contract rather than midnight.

baseline.title: Baseline exit
paragraphs: [[time-baseline-exit|Baseline-exit]] defines the end of the same NYSE session window in which the forecast was opened. The daily label, the honest fact, and the split boundaries all depend on this moment.
It is tied to the real session close and therefore keeps the same meaning across training, backtest, and UI.

terms.description: The actual close of the current NYSE session minus a small technical buffer. This is the canonical end of the baseline window.
A shortened NYSE trading day. On such dates, baseline-exit shifts with the real exchange close.
A small gap before the official close used to keep the time contract stable and repeatable.

baseline.why: Baseline-exit fixes the cause of honest day labeling. If that moment floats, the model starts learning and being evaluated on different windows.
baseline.example: On an [[time-early-close|early close]] day, the correct baseline window must move with the exchange. Otherwise the system compares the forecast with fact on the wrong interval.

dayKeys.title: Day keys and time types
paragraphs: The project separates [[time-entry-day-key|entry day]] and [[time-exit-day-key|exit day]] so that forecast creation is not confused with the day when the baseline window closes.
Exit-day-key defines the honest split boundary. If a convenient calendar date is used instead, train and oos start to mix.

terms.description: The UTC day key of entry time. It tells the system which day the forecast belongs to at creation time.
The UTC day key of baseline exit. It marks the day where the baseline window really ends.
The canonical split boundary based on exit day key. It separates train from oos in the honest time contract.
The UTC timestamp of the baseline-window end used in both split logic and truthful daily labeling.

dayKeys.why: Separating the day keys fixes the cause of time-boundary errors. It stops the code from using a convenient date instead of the date that actually belongs to the exit contract.
dayKeys.example: If split logic is built on [[time-entry-day-key|entry day]] instead of [[time-exit-day-key|exit day]], some days can fall into the wrong segment even though the calendar still looks reasonable.

weekend.title: Weekends and exclusions
paragraphs: NY weekends are excluded from the trading flow not for aesthetics, but for a stable time contract. The model should operate only on business days where its NYSE-based timing logic is actually defined.
If such days are not filtered out, the archive fills with entries that do not have a proper trading context. That pollutes train, weakens split honesty, and makes the UI harder to read correctly.

terms.description: The rule that prevents the creation of a trading day for NY Saturday and NY Sunday.
The distortion introduced when dates without valid NY business context are allowed into the history.

weekend.why: Weekend exclusion removes the cause of weak time signal: the model does not train on days where the system's own trading-time logic is undefined.
weekend.example: If NY weekends enter train without a valid session contract, the archive may look fuller, but the link between features and day movement becomes dirtier.

header.title: Features and indicators
header.subtitle: Which features stand behind the signal and how to read their contribution in the model.

pfiNotes.featureMissing: PFI: feature not found in report.
pfiNotes.summary: PFI across {{count}} models: ΔAUC ≈ {{importance}}; corr ≈ {{corr}}; ΔMean ≈ {{deltaMean}}.

overview.title: How to read features
paragraphs: [[features-feature|A feature]] is a market signal available at forecast time. What matters is which part of the market it describes: impulse, backdrop, volatility, or regime.
[[features-pfi|PFI]] shows how much model quality drops without that information. It must be read together with the meaning of the feature, otherwise the number has no conclusion behind it.

rows: The larger the absolute drop, the more the model depends on that feature.
The relation between feature value and model score.
The sign shows direction of influence and the magnitude shows strength.
Difference of mean values between classes.
Shows which way the feature is shifted across outcomes.

terms.description: A numeric signal the model receives at forecast time as part of the causal market context.
Permutation Feature Importance — a way to measure how much model quality depends on a particular feature.
The AUC change after feature permutation. The stronger the quality drop, the more important the feature is for the model.
The correlation between a feature value and the model score. It shows both direction and stability of the feature influence.
The difference between class means for the same feature.

overview.why: Without the meaning of the feature, even a high PFI stays just a number. Feature reading starts with what the model actually saw in the market.
overview.example: A feature group can have high [[features-delta-auc|ΔAUC]], but that still does not say what became clearer in the market until the feature meaning is understood.

returns.title: Returns and relative dynamics
paragraphs: [[features-returns-sol-ret-30|SolRet30]], [[features-returns-btc-ret-30|BtcRet30]], and [[features-returns-sol-btc-ret-30|SolBtcRet30]] show the broader background and SOL's relative strength against the market.

Short-horizon returns and spreads such as [[features-returns-sol-ret-1|SolRet1]], [[features-returns-sol-ret-3|SolRet3]], [[features-returns-gap-btc-sol-1|GapBtcSol1]], and [[features-returns-gap-btc-sol-3|GapBtcSol3]] show whether SOL is accelerating now and whether its impulse is diverging from BTC right before the forecast.

terms.description: SOL return over the last 30 6h bars.
BTC return over the same horizon.
The difference between medium-horizon SOL return and BTC return. It shows SOL relative strength against the broader market.
SOL return over the last 1 bar.
SOL return over the last 3 bars.
BTC return over the last 1 bar.
BTC return over the last 3 bars.
The one-bar short-horizon gap between BTC and SOL momentum.
The three-bar short-horizon gap between BTC and SOL momentum.

returns.why: This group shows whether the move comes from the whole market or whether SOL already has its own relative strength.
returns.example: If [[features-returns-sol-ret-30|SolRet30]] is strong but [[features-returns-sol-btc-ret-30|SolBtcRet30]] is weak, SOL is rising with the market but not outperforming BTC.

indicators.title: Indicators and macro
paragraphs: [[features-indicators-fng|FngNorm]], [[features-indicators-dxy|DxyChg30]], and [[features-indicators-gold|GoldChg30]] describe the external backdrop of the signal: sentiment, dollar context, and defensive regime.
[[features-indicators-btc-vs-200|BtcVs200]] shows the phase of the main market. The same SOL signal is read differently in a strong BTC backdrop and in a weak one.

terms.description: The normalized Fear & Greed index. It captures broad crypto-market sentiment.
The medium-horizon change in the DXY dollar index.
The medium-horizon move in gold through PAXG. It shows the defensive macro backdrop.
BTC position relative to SMA200. It acts as a long-trend market regime anchor.

indicators.why: The backdrop explains why the same local SOL move can mean different things in different market environments.
indicators.example: Weak [[features-indicators-fng|FngNorm]] together with a worse [[features-indicators-dxy|DxyChg30]] can signal a more nervous environment even if SOL still has a local impulse.

momentum.title: Momentum and volatility
paragraphs: [[features-momentum-rsi|SolRsiCenteredScaled]] and [[features-momentum-rsi-slope|RsiSlope3Scaled]] show whether the impulse is stretched and whether it is still accelerating.
[[features-momentum-atr|AtrPct]] and [[features-momentum-dyn-vol|DynVol]] show how much risk stands behind that move.

terms.description: The centered and scaled RSI(14) feature for SOL.
The short-horizon slope of RSI. It shows whether momentum is accelerating or fading.
ATR(14) normalized by entry price. It shows typical movement amplitude relative to the current price.
Average absolute move over 10 bars. It is a practical measure of current market stress.

momentum.why: The same direction can look very different if the market is calm in one case and already overheated in the other.
momentum.example: If growth momentum is still strong on [[features-momentum-rsi|RSI]] but [[features-momentum-atr|AtrPct]] is also expanding, the model may see a move that is getting more expensive in risk terms.

regime.title: Regimes and EMA
paragraphs: [[features-regime-down|RegimeDownFlag]] and [[features-regime-hard|HardRegimeIs2Flag]] show the broader market backdrop: calm, downward, or harsh.

EMA features such as [[features-regime-sol-above-ema50|SolAboveEma50]], [[features-regime-sol-ema-spread|SolEma50vs200]], and [[features-regime-btc-ema-spread|BtcEma50vs200]] show where SOL and BTC sit relative to their trend anchors.

terms.description: A bearish market-regime flag.
A flag for a harsher market regime where risk and instability are usually higher.
SOL position relative to EMA50. It tells whether price sits above or below a near-trend anchor.
The EMA50/EMA200 spread for SOL. It shows trend strength and direction.
The EMA50/EMA200 spread for BTC. It describes the broad market trend backdrop.

regime.why: Regime features show why the same local signal is read differently in calm and harsh markets.
regime.example: If [[features-regime-down|RegimeDownFlag]] is already on, even a decent local SOL impulse may need to be read more cautiously than it would in a neutral regime.

testsPage.title: Tests and safeguards
testsPage.subtitle: Which checks stop pretty but false statistics from reaching the backtest, API, and UI.

paragraphs: Tests are a set of independent barriers that catch different error classes in data, time boundaries, architecture, and reports.

Checks are split by error type: some catch future data entering the input, others enforce basic integrity of numbers and sections, others keep layer boundaries, and another group checks whether the full calculation still reproduces.
tests-overview.why: A single smoke test shows only a generic failure. Split checks point to the actual contract that broke.
tests-overview.example: A failing architecture test means a broken dependency boundary, while a failing sanity check usually means invalid values or a broken report payload. These are different causes.

terms.description: Future-data leakage into features, labels, or evaluation. It is dangerous because it improves statistics instead of causing an obvious crash.
Validation of basic data and report invariants: ranges, [[tests-sanity-nan|NaN]], [[tests-sanity-infinity|Infinity]], empty sections, and broken boundaries.

paragraphs: [[tests-sanity-core|Sanity checks]] stop empty or broken reports from looking valid. They verify ranges, required sections, [[tests-sanity-nan|NaN]], and [[tests-sanity-infinity|Infinity]].
If the data is missing, the system should fail explicitly. Replacing emptiness with a safe-looking value makes the screen dishonest.
tests-sanity.why: Broken data often looks plausible. If the system does not stop at the first broken contract, the problem becomes visible too late.
tests-sanity.example: If a probability arrives as [[tests-sanity-infinity|Infinity]], the correct behavior is to stop the computation with an invariant error. The wrong behavior is to quietly replace it with `0` and keep rendering.

terms.description: A package of validations for basic data and report correctness. It stops broken state before it reaches the UI.
A principle where the system throws immediately on contract violation instead of hiding the issue behind a safe-looking default.
The numeric value “not a number”. For probabilities, PnL, and metrics it always means the computation is broken.
An infinite numeric value. In reports and probabilities it usually means a broken formula or division by zero and should trigger fail-fast.

paragraphs: [[tests-leakage-core|Leakage]] means future information reaches features or evaluation. That defect makes past statistics look better than they would in real use.
[[tests-leakage-future-blind|Future-blind checks]], [[tests-leakage-shuffle|shuffle tests]], and time boundaries answer one question: could the system know this at decision time?
tests-leakage.why: Leakage is dangerous because it looks not like a crash, but like an unusually strong model.
tests-leakage.example: If quality barely drops after label shuffling, that is not robustness. It is a reason to suspect [[tests-leakage-core|leakage]] or a broken [[tests-leakage-causal-contract|causal contract]].

terms.description: Future information leaking into features, labels, or evaluation. It makes offline statistics better while failing to reproduce in live use.
A test that confirms future data changes do not alter a decision that was already made.
Checks on shuffled labels or data. If quality barely drops, the model may be leaning on leakage rather than on a real signal.
Rule that the decision uses only data available before forecast time. Once that contract breaks, historical evaluation stops being honest.
Rules for [[tests-leakage-train|Train]], [[tests-leakage-oos|OOS]], entry time, and [[tests-leakage-day-key|day keys]]. They preserve historical evaluation honesty.
The training segment of history where the model fits its parameters. By itself it does not prove honest transfer to new days.
The out-of-sample segment of new days after the training boundary. This is the honest slice for transfer validation.
The contractual trading-day identifier that keeps history assigned to the correct time slice instead of mixing neighboring windows.

paragraphs: Performance checks ensure that the full computation stays within its time and memory limits and remains reproducible on the real horizon.
If the full run becomes too heavy, the temptation appears to cut the data horizon or disable barriers. At that point the contract changes, not just the speed.
tests-perf.why: Time and memory control protect repeatability of the full computation. Without them, temporary shortcuts can change behavior more than the slowdown itself.
tests-perf.example: If a refactor makes the full-history report exceed its runtime budget, the team may be tempted to disable an expensive check. That is no longer a neutral optimization.

terms.description: Allowed time and resource cost for the full run. Systematic budget violations create pressure for risky temporary workarounds.
Ability to reproduce the same result on the same data and parameters. Without it, version-to-version comparison stops being trustworthy.

truthfulnessPage.title: Data truthfulness
truthfulnessPage.subtitle: How the project separates an honest forecast from facts that became known later, and how that meaning reaches reports.

labels.truth-causal: What was known before the decision
labels.truth-structure: How the project keeps them separate

paragraphs: Data truthfulness is the contract that keeps separate what was known before the decision, what became known after the window, and how the final result reaches the report.
Forecast and fact should stay side by side without being mixed. The screen should show what the model knew before the trade and what happened later.

rows: [[truth-overview-causal|Causal snapshot]] cuts future-only fields and fixes the moment of data availability.
[[truth-overview-fact|Post-fact outcome]] is computed only after the window closes and never flows back into the decision.
A comparison between forecast and outcome.
[[truth-overview-report|ReportDocument]] stores already assembled meaning for API and UI.
The same meaning in API and UI.
[[truth-overview-trace|Traceability]] preserves source, generation time, and selected slice.
Source and freshness of the report.

truth-overview.why: If these stages are mixed, a pretty screen no longer proves an honest computation.

terms.description: A snapshot of data available at decision time. It freezes what the model truly knew before entry.
The final day outcome after the [[truth-omniscient-window|window]] closes. It is needed for quality and PnL evaluation, but must not alter the [[truth-causal-core|causal decision]].
Unified report contract with sections, tables, and metadata. The UI reads it as a ready-made source of meaning.
Transparent linkage between report source, generation time, selected slice, and the screen.

paragraphs: [[truth-causal-core|Causal layer]] is the decision path that uses only what was already known at entry time.

[[truth-causal-entry|EntryUtc]], [[truth-causal-asof|AsOfUtc]], and [[truth-causal-cutoff|DataCutoffUtc]] fix that knowledge boundary. If later information reaches the input, the forecast becomes dishonest before reports and charts even appear.
truth-causal.why: A broken knowledge boundary corrupts the whole computation, not a single metric.
truth-causal.example: If realized [[truth-causal-minmove|MinMove]] reaches the feature row before the forecast, even a strong [[truth-causal-oos|OOS]] is no longer trustworthy. That is not a strong model; it is a broken [[truth-causal-contract|causal contract]].

terms.description: Decision path that uses only the data truly available before entry. It defines the causally valid forecast.
UTC entry moment from which the allowed information set for the decision layer is derived.
Upper historical boundary the model is allowed to treat as known at decision time.
Data cut-off moment before forecast computation. After it, new candles and derived fields must not enter the [[truth-overview-causal|causal snapshot]].
The set of features and fields the model was actually allowed to use before the forecast. Later data inside the causal input breaks decision honesty.
Rule that the [[truth-causal-core|decision layer]] receives only [[truth-causal-input|causal input]] and never sees post-fact knowledge.
The minimum meaningful daily price move. If realized MinMove reaches the [[truth-causal-input|causal input]] before the forecast, the project is already peeking into the future.
The out-of-sample segment after the training boundary. A pretty OOS is no longer honest once the [[truth-causal-input|causal input]] is polluted by [[truth-overview-fact|post-fact knowledge]].

paragraphs: [[truth-omniscient-core|Omniscient layer]] is the factual path after the result window closes. This is where the project computes what really happened to the trade and how the day ended.
This layer exists to evaluate the past, not to help the decision with hindsight. Its knowledge must never flow back into the [[truth-causal-core|causal path]].
truth-omniscient.why: Without this layer, truthful outcome and PnL evaluation would be impossible. Its knowledge must stay cleanly separated from the decision path.
truth-omniscient.example: Showing forecast [[truth-causal-minmove|MinMove]] next to realized [[truth-causal-minmove|MinMove]] is correct only when the realized value is computed inside the [[truth-omniscient-core|omniscient layer]] after the window closes rather than feeding the live decision.

terms.description: Post-fact evaluation path that sees the final outcome after the window closes and is used for outcome, PnL, and diagnostics.
Period after entry during which the market realizes the actual day outcome. Before it closes, the post-fact truth does not yet exist honestly.
Record of the [[truth-overview-fact|factual outcome]] and execution context inside the post-fact path.

paragraphs: Project layers separate decision logic, post-fact evaluation, and report delivery.
That split exists so forecast, fact, and the UI contract cannot be mixed by accident in one place.

rows: Owns the decision and causally valid features.
So post-fact knowledge cannot leak into live logic.
Computes outcome, PnL, and factual execution after the window.
So evaluation stays richer without changing the decision.
Transfers DTOs across layers and scenarios.
So the meaning of a field is not reinvented at every pipeline hop.
So the UI does not invent business meaning from internal structures.

truth-structure.why: If decision, fact, and report delivery live in one layer, the boundary between forecast and result quickly disappears.
truth-structure.example: In this architecture, [[truth-structure-interop|Interop]] preserves the same field meaning across [[truth-structure-orchestration|orchestration]], [[truth-structure-reports|reports]], and [[truth-structure-api|API]].

terms.description: Solution project for causally valid decision logic. It must not know post-fact outcomes.
Solution project for post-fact evaluation and factual result computation.
DTO and contract layer between projects. It preserves field meaning across [[truth-structure-causal|causal]], [[truth-structure-reports|reports]], and [[truth-structure-api|API]].
Scenario orchestration layer that coordinates flows between [[truth-structure-causal|causal]], [[truth-structure-omniscient|omniscient]], and [[truth-structure-reports|reports]].
Project that assembles report documents into a stable contract for API and UI.
HTTP delivery layer for reports and current prediction. It should return a valid contract or an explicit error.

paragraphs: Ready-made meaning reaches API and UI through a fixed delivery path.

After the computation, meaning must not change on the way to API and UI. The result is stored in [[truth-overview-report|ReportDocument]], read through [[truth-delivery-storage|ReportStorage]], and delivered via stable [[truth-delivery-endpoint|API endpoints]].
The UI should read a ready contract instead of guessing missing pieces. If the report or section is missing, that is an error, not an empty screen.
truth-delivery.why: Meaning can still break on the very last meter if the UI starts inventing data on behalf of the backend.
truth-delivery.example: If the backend did not return the required section, the correct behavior is a contract error. The wrong behavior is an empty table that pretends the strategy simply had nothing to trade.

terms.description: Storage of reports by `kind/id`, used by the API to fetch ready-made documents and preserve source traceability.
Report generation timestamp used for [[truth-overview-trace|traceability]] and freshness checks.
Contract route for forecast or report reading. It should return structured data or an explicit error.

paragraphs: Independent barriers protect data truthfulness after the computation.
Truthfulness does not rest on one check, but on several independent barriers: some watch architecture, others future data, others time boundaries, and others broken results after the run.
Each barrier catches its own error class. One test cannot catch both a broken number and an illegal dependency between layers.

rows: Shows that a pretty metric may actually be caused by leakage.
Empty sections, broken values, and contradictory report invariants.
Stops corrupted documents before they reach API and UI.

truth-tests.why: One barrier cannot see every error class. A set of independent checks is required.
truth-tests.example: If the UI still looks clean but [[truth-tests-arch|ArchTests]] show a [[truth-structure-causal|causal]] -> [[truth-structure-omniscient|omniscient]] dependency, those numbers are no longer trustworthy: the architecture defect compromises the final data.

terms.description: Architecture dependency tests across project layers. They ensure [[truth-structure-causal|causal]], [[truth-structure-omniscient|omniscient]], [[truth-structure-reports|reports]], and [[truth-structure-orchestration|orchestration]] do not mix responsibility.
Checks for expected degradation on randomized data. If quality does not drop, the project suspects [[truth-tests-leakage|leakage]] or a broken [[truth-causal-contract|causal contract]].
Rules that fix [[truth-tests-train|Train]], [[truth-tests-oos|OOS]], [[truth-causal-entry|EntryUtc]], and [[truth-tests-baseline-exit|baseline-exit]] boundaries for honest historical evaluation.
Post-run validation package that checks the report result after computation completes and blocks broken documents from [[truth-delivery-storage|ReportStorage]] and [[truth-structure-api|API]].
Future information leaking into features or evaluation. That defect makes past statistics look better than they would in real use.
The numeric value “not a number”. If it reaches a report or UI, the computation has already broken a basic data invariant.
The training slice of history. It is needed for fitting, but it is not proof of honest quality by itself.
The out-of-sample slice of new days after the train boundary. It is needed for honest transfer validation.
The contractual moment when the daily window is considered closed. It anchors fact labeling, split boundaries, and historical evaluation honesty.
